name: Dr Duan Biggs
role: The Charles Olajos and Ted Goslow Chair for Southwestern Environmental Science and Policy
focus: Conservation policy • Human-wildlife coexistence • Tourism resilience
bio: Dr. Duan Biggs is the Olajos-Goslow Chair of Environmental Science and Policy and Associate Professor in NAU's School of Earth and Sustainability, where he joined the faculty in August 2021. He is the founder and leader of Resilient Conservation, an organization focused on developing innovative approaches to conservation challenges in the Anthropocene. Born in Namibia and raised near South Africa's Kruger National Park, he brings deep personal experience with human-wildlife coexistence and African conservation to research that integrates scientific rigor with stakeholder engagement to deliver real-world policy and on-the-ground impact. Dr. Biggs earned his Ph.D. in Marine Biology/Coral Reef Studies from James Cook University and his M.Sc. in Conservation Biology from the University of Cape Town. His scholarship spans conservation policy, community-based responses to illegal wildlife trade, tourism resilience, and global standards for human-wildlife coexistence, and has been cited more than 8,000 times—helping shape over $350 million in donor and government funding. He founded and co-chairs the IUCN World Commission on Protected Areas Task Force on Human-Wildlife Coexistence and serves as a policy advisor to organizations including WWF and the Luc Hoffmann Institute. At NAU, he teaches courses linking remote sensing technology, global biodiversity datasets, and community-based conservation, mentoring students through applied research partnerships with organizations such as Conservation International, the IUCN, and the Grand Canyon Trust.
